title: 3 of the Best Dividend Stocks to Buy in May 2026
author: David Jagielski, Cpa
description: The article recommends three dividend stocks for May 2026: AbbVie (yielding 3.4% with strong growth despite Humira patent loss), Verizon Communications (yielding 5.9% with growth prospects from Frontier acquisition), and Canadian Natural Resources (yielding 3.8% with 26 consecutive years of dividend increases). All three stocks trade at low valuations and offer stable income with growth potential.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Strong dividend growth (33% over 5 years), successful business expansion post-Allergan acquisition, robust drug portfolio, revenue growth of 12% in Q1 2026, and attractive valuation (P/E of 14) despite 9% year-to-date decline.

Highest dividend yield at 5.9%, strategic Frontier acquisition expanding fiber access and growth opportunities, stable business model, 13% dividend increase over 5 years, and low forward P/E multiple under 10.

Consistent dividend payer with 26 consecutive years of increases and ~20% compounded annual growth rate, strong position to benefit from elevated oil prices, 40% stock price increase year-to-date, and modest valuation with P/E under 14.

title: Why US Energy Stocks and Gold Could Be the Biggest Winners Ahead
author: Frank Holmes
description: Following Middle East hostilities, a significant oil price divergence has emerged between Western benchmarks and Middle Eastern markets, with Oman crude hitting record $173/barrel. While the U.S. is better insulated due to strong domestic production and strategic reserves, Europe faces severe risks with natural gas storage at five-year lows. The article identifies U.S. energy stocks and gold as primary investment opportunities, with energy producers benefiting from higher oil prices and gold positioned to benefit from fiscal pressures and stagflation risks.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Identified by Goldman Sachs as a top oil producer with favorable risk-reward profiles; benefits from elevated oil prices and strong domestic production positioning
Named as a top oil pick by Goldman Sachs; positioned to capture additional revenue from higher oil prices, with domestic producers capturing ~$400 billion in additional revenue at $130 oil

title: Oil Is Down Today, Up Tomorrow. Here's Why I'm Not Worried.
author: Matt Dilallo
description: The author discusses his investment in three oil stocks (Chevron, ConocoPhillips, and Canadian Natural Resources) that can benefit from both rising and falling oil prices. Despite recent volatility caused by Iran-U.S. military tensions, he remains confident these companies have low breakeven costs, strong dividend growth histories, and can generate substantial cash flow even at $70/barrel oil prices.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Strong dividend history (39 years of increases), generates $600M additional earnings per $1 oil price increase, maintains 10%+ annual free cash flow growth at $70 oil, and has attractive 3.43% dividend yield

Decade of dividend increases, can double free cash flow by 2029 at $70 oil, low breakeven costs in the $40s, and provides upside to higher crude prices with $100M+ earnings increase per $1 oil price rise
Extended dividend streak to 26 consecutive years, low oil breakeven levels in the $40s, can thrive at lower oil prices, and provides portfolio upside during higher crude price environments

title: Stock Market Today, March 19: Brent Crude's $119 Spike Rattles Markets
author: Emma Newbery
description: U.S. stock markets declined on March 19, 2026, as Brent crude oil spiked above $119/barrel, intensifying inflation concerns and Middle East conflict fears. Energy stocks gained while tech and industrial sectors weakened. Gold prices fell sharply, dragging down mining stocks. JPMorgan Chase cut its S&P 500 year-end target, warning that elevated oil prices could slow global growth.

title: 9,300% Dividend Growth Since 2001: Is This $39 Stock the Answer to Income Investors' Prayers?
author: William Dahl
description: Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) has delivered exceptional dividend growth of 9,300% since 2001, with an average annual increase of 21%. The oil and gas company generates $14.8 billion in operating cash flow, easily covering its $3.6 billion dividend while maintaining profitability even at oil prices above $21 per barrel. Despite potential energy market headwinds, the company's 4.33% dividend yield significantly outpaces the S&P 500 average.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: The company demonstrates exceptional dividend growth history (9,300% since 2001), strong cash flow generation ($14.8B operating cash flow), sustainable dividend coverage, and attractive yield (4.33%) relative to market averages. The company maintains profitability at low oil price thresholds ($21/barrel), providing downside protection even in adverse market conditions.
